coredata.dc:title: Facile synthesis of highly efficient photocatalysts based on organic small molecular co-catalyst
coredata.dc:creator: Sun, Youyi
Wang, Congxu
Guo, Guizheng
Fu, Qiang
Xiong, Zhiyuan
Li, Dan
Liu, Yaqing
coredata.dc:description: A new metal-free organic molecular as co-catalyst was developed, which could drastically enhance the photocatalytic performance of photocatalyst (eg. g-C3N4, MoS2 and TiO2). The enhancing photocatalytic activity was evaluated for photocatalytic degradation of organic dyes. It showed ultra-high photocatalytic rate (4.17 mg/g·min) and ultra-short time (about 6.0 min) for photocatalytic degradation of organic dyes (25 mg/ml). The enhanced photocatalytic performance was attributed to suppress recombination of photogenerated charges and provide a new photoredox reaction pathway under molecular co-catalyst assistance. The study represents a facile method to develop ultra-effectively photocatalyst for applications in production of green and renew-able energy carrier, H2 from water, reduction of CO2, synthesis of fine chemicals and remediation of environmental pollutants.
coredata.dcterms:subject: g-C3N4
Molecular co-catalysts
Hole transfer
Organic molecular radicals
Photocatalytic degradation

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of oxalic acid modified graphitic carbon nitride (OA@g-C3N4)

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) XRD patterns of (a) g-C3N4 and (b) OA@g-C3N4. (B) IR spectra of g-C3N4 with various mass ratio of OA/g-C3N4, (a) 0, (b) 0.25, (c) 0.5, (d) 1.0, (e) 2.0, (f) 3.0, (g) 4.0 and (h) 5.0, (C) IR spectrum of pure OA, (D) TG curve of OA@g-C3N4 with mass ratio of 5.0.
(A) XPS survey spectra of OA@g-C3N4, high-resolution XPS spectra of (B) C1s, (C) N1s and (D) of O1s of OA@g-C3N4.
TEM images of (A) pure g-C3N4 and (B) OA@g-C3N4, SEM images of (C) pure g-C3N4 and (D) OA@g-C3N4, (E) the EDS spectra of OA@g-C3N4, the inset of (E) is the elemental mapping images of OA@g-C3N4.

(A) Degradation efficiency on bulk g-C3N4 with different mass ratio of OA/g-C3N4, (B) UV–vis spectra of RhB solution with various irradiation times under outdoor sun light, (C) UV–vis spectra of RhB solution with various times under dark, The inset of (B) is the optical photograph of a photocatalytic degradation experiment.

(A) Completely photocatalytic degradation time as function of initial RhB concentration, (B) SDR of photocatalysts based on g-C3N4 for degradation of RhB, (C) A magnified SDR of photocatalysts based on g-C3N4 region of (B) marked in orange. (D) Cycle stability test on OA@g-C3N4 photocatalysts.

(A) XRD pattern and (B) IR spectra of OA@g-C3N4 (a) before and (b) after three cycling photocatalytic experiments. (C) TEM image, (D) SEM image and (E) the elemental mapping images of OA@g-C3N4 after three cycling photocatalytic experiments.
HPLC spectra of RhB (initial concentration 25.0 mg/l) and the intermediate products during the photocatalytic process (OA@g-C3N4 with mass ratio of 4, 50.0 mg) as function of photocatalytic time.

(A) Degradation efficiency of RhB solution using g-C3N4-based (co)-catalysts with various acids. (B) Optical photograph of RhB solution as function of photocatalytic time for g-C3N4 with addition of (a) oxalic acid, (b) trifluoroacetic acid and (c) acetic acid.
(A) UV-DRS and (B) (Ahυ)1/2 versus hυ curve of g-C3N4 with various mass ratio of OA/g-C3N4. (a) 0, (b) 0.25, (c) 0.5, (d) 1.0, (e) 2.0, (f) 3.0 and (g) 4.0.
(A) PL spectra and (B) Transient photocurrent curves of g-C3N4 with various mass ratio of OA/g-C3N4, (a) 0, (b) 0.25, (c) 0.5, (d) 1.0, (e) 2.0, (f) 3.0 and (g) 4.0.
Degradation efficiency on (A) pure g-C3N4 and (B) OA@g-C3N4 in the (a) absence and (b) presence of ammonium oxalate.

XRD patterns of (A) pure OA@MoS2 and (B) OA@TiO2. (C) Degradation efficiency of (a) MoS2 and (b) OA@MoS2 as function of photocatalytic time under visible light irradiation, (D) Degradation efficiency of (a) TiO2 and OA@TiO2 catalysts as function of photocatalytic time under visible light irradiation.
Schematic illustration of the photocatalytic degradation mechanism in (A) absence and (B) presence of organic molecular co-catalyst.

head.ce:title: Facile synthesis of highly efficient photocatalysts based on organic small molecular co-catalyst

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Shanxi Province Key Laboratory of Functional Nanocomposites, North University of China, Taiyuan 030051, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Shanxi Province Key Laboratory of Functional Nanocomposites

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemical and Bio-molecular Engineering, The University of Melbourne, Victoria 3010, Australia

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemical and Bio-molecular Engineering

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: Shanxi Province Key Laboratory of Functional Nanocomposites, North University of China, Taiyuan 030051, PR China (Y.Y. Sun).

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Shanxi Province Key Laboratory of Functional Nanocomposites

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A new metal-free organic molecular as co-catalyst was developed, which could drastically enhance the photocatalytic performance of photocatalyst (eg. g-C3N4, MoS2 and TiO2). The enhancing photocatalytic activity was evaluated for photocatalytic degradation of organic dyes. It showed ultra-high photocatalytic rate (4.17 mg/g·min) and ultra-short time (about 6.0 min) for photocatalytic degradation of organic dyes (25 mg/ml). The enhanced photocatalytic performance was attributed to suppress recombination of photogenerated charges and provide a new photoredox reaction pathway under molecular co-catalyst assistance. The study represents a facile method to develop ultra-effectively photocatalyst for applications in production of green and renew-able energy carrier, H2 from water, reduction of CO2, synthesis of fine chemicals and remediation of environmental pollutants.

ce:section.ce:para: Environmental pollution and sustainable supply of greener energy are two of the main global challenges of the current era [1]. Considering the Sun as an almost inexhaustible and primary source of energy, lately, there are lots of interests in developing visible-light-driven photocatalyst systems for harvesting solar energy to produce cleaner fuel sand resolve the environmental issues [2,3]. Lately, visible light active photocatalysts are getting enormous attention for applications to environment and energy sectors. However, after nearly two decades of research, photocatalyst has been few applied in practical industry [3–5]. This is fundamentally due to a high charge recombination rate of visible-light-driven photocatalysts, leading to low photocatalytic activity [6].

In the past decade, a great deal of efforts has been made to suppress recombination of electrons and holes. These approaches included the developments of co-catalysts, co-deposition of noble metals, doping, surface hybridization and combination systems [7–11]. Among these approaches, development of co-catalysts is one of the most effective strategies to efficient electron-hole separation and transportation, which endowed them with desired properties superior to their individual counterparts [12]. Currently, the co-catalysts were mainly made of noble metals, rare metals or metal oxide semiconductors, which faced several challenging issues for photocatalytic degradation applications. First, the co-catalysts of noble metals, rare metals or metal oxide semiconductors suffered expensive and harmful to environment shortcoming. Second, the loading of inorganic co-catalysts was potentially limited due to be inefficient absorption of solar light, resulting in low efficient electron-hole separation. In addition, these inorganic materials of g-C3N4, MoS2 and TiO2 have received splendid attention for application in photocatalyst due to its low cost, environmentally compatible composition and excellent structural stability [13–17].

Therefore, a new cost-effective and environmental friendly co-catalyst (eg. organic oxalic acid) was developed. Furthermore, the photocatalytic activity of traditional photocatalyst (eg. g-C3N4, MoS2 and TiO2) with the co-catalyst was evaluated for application in photocatalytic degradation of organic pollutants. The present co-catalytic system exhibited the highest specific degradation rate (indicating highest photocatalytic activity) comparing to the results reported in open literature. The co-catalytic system utilizing organic acids represents a novel, simple and highly effective approach to develop advanced photocatalysts for application in production of green and renew-able energy carrier, H2 from water, reduction of CO2, synthesis of fine chemicals and remediation of environmental pollutants.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of oxalic acid modified graphitic carbon nitride (OA@g-C3N4)
ce:section.ce:para: The graphitic carbon nitride was firstly prepared according to previous work [18]. 10.0 g urea was put into a ceramics crucible with cover and heated to 500.0 °C for 3 h at a heating rate of 2.0 °C/min in a muffle furnace. After the reaction, the crucible was taken out and cooled to room temperature. The resultant yellow products milled into a fine power. And then the OA@g-C3N4 was further prepared by the following process. Firstly, a specified mass (12.5 mg, 25.0 mg, 50.0 mg, 100.0 mg, 150.0 mg, 200.0 mg and 250.0 mg) of oxalic acid was mixed with 50 ml g-C3N4 aqueous solution (1.0 mg/ml) under vigorously stirring. Secondly, the mixture was stirred for 1.0 h at room temperature. Thirdly, the products were collected by centrifugation, washed three times with deionized water and dried overnight at 60 °C. In a comparison, OA@TiO2 and OA@MoS2 was further prepared by the above similar process, in which the TiO2 and MoS2 was prepared according to previous works [17,19].
An X-ray diffractometer (HAOYUAN) using Cu Kα (λ = 0.1546 nm) to characterize obtained samples.

The morphology and size of composites were observed by scanning electron microscopy (SEM, JEOL, JSM-6701F) with energy-dispersive spectra and transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEM-2010) at an acceleration voltage of 200.0 kV.
Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) was recorded on a FT-IR spectrophotometer (Thermo Nicolet 360).
UV absorption spectrum and UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra (UV–vis DRS) were obtained on a cary 5000 spectrophotometer.
The fluorescence was recorded on a fluoroscopic photometer (DF 2000).

High performance liquid chromatograph (HPLC) was conducted on an Ultimate 3000 instrument. The solvent was water and methanol mixing solution (75:25), the current velocity of solvent was 0.8 ml/min, column temperature was 50.0 °C and the injection volume was 20.0 μl.

The photocatalytic activity of the g-C3N4 (or TiO2 and MoS2) with co-catalyst OA was evaluated by degrading organic dyes (eg. RhB) under irradiation of a 500.0 W halogen lamp. Typically, 50.0 mg photocatalysts was added into a 50 ml solution of organic dye (15–45.0 mg/l) and magnetically stirred in dark for 1.0 h to attain an adsorption-absorption equilibrium. Then photocatalytic activity of the co-catalytic system was evaluated under visible light irradiation. At certain time intervals, a 4.0 ml suspension was taken out, where the photocatalyst was removed by centrifugal method. Finally, the filtrates were analyzed by recording variations of the absorption band maximum at 553.0 nm in the UV–vis spectra of RhB.

Currently, there were urgent needs for the development of green, low cost, metal-free and efficient photocatalysts for degradation applications. Graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4) has attracted splendid attention due to its visible light absorption, non-toxicity, earth-abundant nature and structural stability [20]. In this study, g-C3N4 was synthesized by a facile thermal decomposition method from urea and used to evaluate the ability of molecular co-catalyst to promote hole transfer. The formation of g-C3N4 and OA@g-C3N4 was confirmed by the XRD pattern and IR spectra as shown in Fig. 1 A and B, respectively. The two samples clearly showed similar two characteristic peaks of 13.1° and 27.5° as shown in Fig. 1A, which were ascribed to the (1 0 0) and (0 0 2) planes of g-C3N4, respectively[20]. The result indicated the formation of g-C3N4 and that the introduction of OA was slight effect on the phase structure of g-C3N4. As seen in Fig. 1B, all samples showed similar IR absorption spectrum. The absorption peak around 3160.0 cm−1 was assigned to the stretching vibration of NH [17]. At the same time, five characteristic peaks at 881.0 cm−1, 1223.0 cm−1, 1316.0 cm−1, 1550.0 cm−1 and 1620.0 cm−1 were attributed to the skeletal vibrations of tristriazine ring units of g-C3N4 [21]. The signal at 1390.0 cm−1 was assigned to the stretching vibrations of s-triazinering units [21]. These results indicated that striazine and tristriazine ring units simultaneously existed in the g-C3N4 prepared using urea precursors. In a comparison, they presented some new absorption peaks for the OA@g-C3N4 samples. For example, the new absorption peaks around 1660.0 cm−1 and 3405.0 cm−1 was assigned to the CO and OH group of OA, respectively [22]. In addition, the absorption peak around 3160.0 cm−1 assigned to the NH group of g-C3N4 was weaker comparing to pure g-C3N4 sample. And the absorption peak around 1660.0 cm−1 and 3405.0 cm−1 assigned to the CO and OH group of OA was shift comparing to pure OA sample (in Fig. 1C). These results indicated a preferential adsorption of OA on the surface of g-C3N4 through the formation of hydrogen bonding between NH of g-C3N4 and COO− of OA. However, the mass ratio of OA/g-C3N4 was larger than 4.0, and a weaker red shift was observed. This result thus confirmed the saturated adsorption of OA molecules on the surface of g-C3N4 at larger mass ratio of OA/g-C3N4 than 4.0. Fig. 1D shows thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) of OA@g-C3N4 with mass ratio of 5.0. The sample had two decomposition step in the temperature range of room temperature to 500.0 °C. The first and second decomposition step was attributed to vaporization of water and thermal degradation of OA adsorbed on surface of g-C3N4, respectively. In addition, the R500 percentage was about 18.6 wt% due to the presence of g-C3N4, which was almost consistent with result of weight method (18.0 wt%). This result thus confirmed the saturated adsorption of OA molecules on the surface of g-C3N4 at larger mass ratio of OA/g-C3N4 than 4.0.

Fig. 2 A shows the survey XPS spectrum of OA@g-C3N4, indicating the presence of carbon, nitrogen and oxygen. In Fig. 2B, high-resolution C1s spectrum of OA@g-C3N4 could be deconvoluted into two species of CC (284.7 eV) and sp2-hybridized carbon in NC (288.2 eV)[13]. Fig. 2C showed high-resolution N1s spectrum with three species peaks at 398.8 eV, 400.0 eV and 404.3 eV [13]. The signal peak at 398.8 eV was attributed to electrons originated from sp2-hybridized nitrogen atoms in CNC, which was in good agreement with FT-IR analysis. The main peaks at 400.0 eV was attributed to the tertiary nitrogen (N(C)3) and the amino functions carrying hydrogen (CNH) of g-C3N4, reflecting structural defects and incomplete condensation in the process of cyanamide polycondensation [13]. The weak peak at 404.3 eV was assigned to π-π excitation. In Fig. 2D, high-resolution O1s spectrum of OA@g-C3N4 was also deconvoluted into two species of CO and OH [14]. This result further confirmed the formation of OA@g-C3N4.

The micro-structure of the g-C3N4 and OA@g-C3N4 was further characterized by SEM and TEM images as shown in Fig. 3 . As shown in Fig. 3A and B, a typical layered platelet-like morphology for pure g-C3N4 and OA@g-C3N4 was observed. In addition, the pure g-C3N4 and OA@g-C3N4 both showed large amounts of aggregation with layered structure as shown in Fig. 3C and D, respectively. The elemental mapping images and energy-dispersive X-ray spectrometer (EDS) of OA@g-C3N4 were shown in Fig. 3E. The elements, e.g. C, N and O were obviously detected and uniformly distributed within the sample, in which the O element was assigned to OA. The weight ratio of C, N and O element was about 31.9:13.4:54.7 according to EDS results. The OA content of OA@g-C3N4 was calculated to be 81.5 wt% according to the weight ratio of these elements, which was almost similar with OA@g-C3N4 with mass weight of 5.0. These results further confirmed the formation of bulk g-C3N4 and OA@g-C3N4. At the same time, these results also indicated that the saturated adsorption of OA molecules on the surface of g-C3N4 at larger mass ratio of OA/g-C3N4 than 4.0.

Photocatalytic performance of g-C3N4 with different amounts of OA was investigated and compared by degrading RhB in aqueous solution under visible light irradiation as shown in Fig. 4 A. Notably, degradation efficiency of OA@g-C3N4 increased with increasing in OA/g-C3N4 mass ratio from 0.25 to 4.0, reaching a maximum value of 99.8% for 12.0 min. This result was three times higher than that (ca. 33%) of bare g-C3N4. The degradation efficiency of OA@g-C3N4 slightly changed at higher OA/g-C3N4 mass ratio of 4.0. This may be attributed to the saturated adsorption of OA molecules on the surface of g-C3N4, which was confirmed by IR spectra as shown in Fig. 1B. The degradation experiments under sun light irradiation (Location: North University of China, N37.998, E112.4726; Date: 14/05/2017; Weather: Cloudy.) was carried out to further confirm the photocatalytic activity of the OA@g-C3N4 catalytic system as shown in inset of Fig. 4B. It shows the UV–vis spectra of RhB solution with various irradiation times under outdoor sun light. It clearly showed that the RhB solution with high concentration (25.0 mg/l) could be quickly degraded by OA@g-C3N4 under sun light irradiation for 16.0 min. The phenomenon was difficult to be observed in previous works [23–26], in which the RhB solution with high concentration (25.0 mg/l) needed long time (>1.0 h) to completely degraded under sun light irradiation. The problem restricted its practical applications. These results indicated that the OA@g-C3N4 was expected to applied in photocatalytic degradation of dyes in the real-world samples by using nature sun light. In a comparison, the degradation and adsorption of RhB under dark in the presence of the photocatalyst was also characterized as shown in Fig. 4C. It clearly showed that the UV–vis spectra of RhB solution were few change in the presence of photocatalyst. The result confirmed that the degradation and adsorption of RhB was negligible under dark in the presence of the photocatalyst.

There were many factors to affect degradation efficiency, such as initial concentration of dyes, amount of photocatalysts, irradiation time and so on [27,28]. Therefore, it was difficult to determine and compare the photocatalytic activities of different photocatalysts by the degradation efficiency. Here, the specific degradation rate (SDR, mg/g·min) was proposed to evaluate the photocatalytic activity of different photocatalysts under irradiation with same radiant power density.
SDR =
V × (
C 0
-
C t
)
M × T
where V is volume of pollutants solution (l), C0 (mg/l) and Ct (mg/l) are the concentration of pollutants solution before and after treatment, respectively. M (g) is the amount of applied photocatalysts and T (min) is the irradiation time. The minimum irradiation time required for complete degradation of RhB using OA@g-C3N4 co-catalysts was plotted versus various initial concentrations of pollutant solution as shown in Fig. 5 A. The corresponding SDR of OA@g-C3N4 photocatalysts was also calculated as shown in Fig. 5B. It clearly shows that the SDR decreased from 3.0 mg/g·min to 1.0 mg/g·min with the increase of initial RhB concentration from 15.0 mg/l to 45.0 mg/l. Surprisingly, the present OA@g-C3N4 photocatalytic system exhibited the highest SDR in comparison with other photocatalytic systems for degradation of RhB reported in previous works [7–9,20,21,27–44,45–48]. In addition, the SDR of the present system was almost 10 times higher than the results reported in previous literature at same initial concentration of RhB as shown in Fig. 4C [7,21,27–35,40–42,44]. These results further confirmed that the photocatalytic activity of g-C3N4 could be significantly improved by introducing a co-catalyst OA.

The stability and reusability of the OA@g-C3N4 photocatalysts were important factor from the viewpoint of practical applications and have been evaluated through three cycles experiments using recovered OA@g-C3N4 photocatalysts. After three cycles under uniform conditions, there was no apparent deactivation of the SDR over OA@g-C3N4 for photocatalytic degradation of RhB as shown in Fig. 5D. Furthermore, the similar XRD patterns and IR spectra of OA@g-C3N4 were identical before and after three consecutive cycles as shown in Fig. 6 A and B, respectively. TEM and SEM measurements also showed a similar morphology for recovered OA@g-C3N4 after three consecutive cycles as shown in Fig. 6C and D, respectively. These results indicated that the phase structure and morphology of g-C3N4 was slight change after three consecutive cycles. The elemental mapping images of OA@g-C3N4 after three cycles were also characterized as shown in Fig. 6E, revealing O elements to be uniformly distributed on the g-C3N4. This result indicates that the OA molecules were adsorbed on the surface of g-C3N4 and recovered together with g-C3N4 through centrifuging after photocatalytic process, without degradation. These results confirmed the good stability and reusability of the OA@g-C3N4 photocatalysts.

The above result was further confirmed by the HPLC spectra of RhB aqueous solution as function of various irradiation time as shown in Fig. 7 . It clearly showed that the intensity of peak at 8.1 min assigned to RhB obviously decreased with increasing irradiation time and completely disappeared after 12.0 min of irradiation. At the same time, the intensity of peak at 6.0 min assigned to OA was identical when increasing irradiation time, indicating the concentration of OA remains constant during the whole photocatalytic process. These results further confirmed that the OA@g-C3N4 photocatalyst could ultra-effective degradation of dye and the good stability and reusability of the OA@g-C3N4 photocatalysts.

It was well-known that photocatalytic activity was strongly dependent on the phase structure, surface morphology, energy-band structure parameter and the separation efficiency of photogenerated charges [27]. To disclose the role of OA molecules in the photocatalytic degradation reaction, a series of control experiments were carried out for comparison. As seen from the above XRD, SEM and TEM results, the crystal phase structure and morphology of g-C3N4 has not evidently changed after the combination with OA. These results demonstrated that the phase structure and surface morphology were slightly responsible for the improvement of photocatalytic activity. To further disclose the essentials of co-catalyst OA, the similar organic molecules (eg. trifluoroacetic acid (TFA) and acetic acid (AA)) were also investigated. When comparing to g-C3N4, AA@g-C3N4 presented identical degradation efficiency, OA@g-C3N4 showed significantly improved degradation efficiency, and TFA@g-C3N4 exhibited moderate enhancement as shown in 8A. The above result was further confirmed by the optical photographs of RhB solution as function of photocatalytic degradation time as shown in Fig. 8 B. It clearly showed the RhB solution completely changed to colorless solution after 12.0 min for the OA@g-C3N4 photocatalyst. In contrast, the color of RhB solution slightly changed under visible light irradiation for 12.0 min. These results confirmed that the pH of solution was also slightly responsible for improving photocatalytic activity by the co-catalyst OA.

The optical properties and energy-band structure of OA@g-C3N4 were also measured. The g-C3N4 and OA@g-C3N4 photocatalyst showed similar identical UV–vis absorption spectra in the wavelength range of 220–800.0 nm as shown in Fig. 9 A. Especially, the visible absorption band (ca. 400–700 nm) of g-C3N4 slightly increased after introduction of OA. The optical band gap (Eg) of g-C3N4 and OA@g-C3N4 was estimated from the intercept of the tangents to the plots of (Ahυ)1/2 versus photo energy as shown in Fig. 9B. All samples showed similar Eg values of 2.8 eV. These results suggested that the light absorption and optical band gap (Eg) of g-C3N4 basically remained and slightly changed after combination with OA. It also demonstrated that the adsorption ability and optical band gap were also slightly responsible for improving photocatalytic activity of g-C3N4 by the introduction of co-catalyst OA.

Fig. 10 A shows the photoluminescence (PL) spectra of pure g-C3N4 and OA@g-C3N4 with various contents. The g-C3N4 exhibited an emission peak centered at approximately 412.0 nm. However, OA@g-C3N4 displayed obvious PL quenching. As well-known, the PL emission originated from the recombination of the excited electron-hole pairs. This result thus confirmed that the OA could greatly suppress recombination of the photogenerated electron-hole pairs [7]. In particular, the PL peak intensity of OA@g-C3N4 decreased with increase in OA content until reached plateau at high OA content (mass ratio of OA@g-C3N4 > 3), which was in agreement with the result obtained from photocatalytic experiments. For the purpose of further exploring the separation and transfer of the charge carriers, the photocurrent experiments (PC) of pure g-C3N4 and OA@g-C3N4 with various contents were conducted under visible light irradiation as shown in Fig. 10B. The photocurrent intensity of OA@g-C3N4 increased with increase in OA content. The PC intensity of OA@g-C3N4 with weight ratio of 4.0 was approximately 2.5 times higher than that of pure g-C3N4. This result further confirmed a higher separation efficiency of electron-hole pairs in OA@g-C3N4 [7,8]. We thus hypothesized that the adsorption of OA on the surface of g-C3N4 could effectively promote the separation efficiency of electron-hole pairs by suppressing their recombination, resulting in improvement of photocatalytic performance.

To gain in-depth insight into the hole charge transfer and
OA− radicals photoredox reaction, the photocatalytic degradation of RhB by OA@g-C3N4 was carried out in presence and absence of hole scavengers. Under visible light irradiation the addition of ammonium oxalate (AO), a hole scavengers, to pure g-C3N4 photocatalyst would not affect the degradation efficiency of dye, suggesting that hole and
OH radicals was minoractive species in photocatalytic process of RhB as shown in Fig. 11 A [7]. In a contrast, it was observed that the addition of AO significantly inhibited the degradation of RhB by 44.5% for OA@g-C3N4 photocatalyst. The result confirmed that
OA− radicals was the predominant reactive species in the photocatalytic reaction of RhB as shown in Fig. 11B. Above results confirmed that hole could be effectively separated and transferred to the surface of g-C3N4 by the addition of co-catalyst OA, suppressing recombination of electron-hole pairs. Furtherance, it also confirmed that reversible
OA− radicals provided new photoredox reaction for degradation of organic dyes, facilitating surface photoredox reaction of photocatalyst.

TiO2 and MoS2 were two of the most popular photocatalysts for decomposition of organic compounds due to their strong photocatalytic activity, chemical and biological inertness and high photochemical stability. Here, TiO2 and MoS2 were prepared and chosen to further evaluate the co-catalytic activity of OA. Fig. 12 A and B shows the XRD of OA@TiO2 and OA@MoS2 products, respectively. As seen in Fig. 12A, four characteristic peaks at 16°, 33°, 40° and 58° were clearly observed, which were assigned to the (0 0 2), (1 0 0), (1 0 3) and (1 1 0) planes of MoS2, respectively (JCPDS Card o. 37-1492) [19]. It clearly showed eight characteristic peaks at 25.4°, 38.0°, 48°, 54.2°, 55.4°, 62.8°, 70.6° and 75.3°, which were assigned to the (1 0 1), (0 0 4), (2 0 0), (1 0 5), (2 1 1), (2 0 4), (2 2 0) and (2 1 5) planes of anatase TiO2, respectively (JCPDS Card o. 84-1286) [17]. These results indicated the formation of OA@TiO2 and OA@MoS2, in which the introduction of OA was slight effect on phrase structure of TiO2 and MoS2. As expected, the degradation efficiency of OA@TiO2 and OA@MoS2 was improved by 5.1 times and 1.6 times comparing to pure TiO2 and MoS2 as shown in Fig. 12C and D, respectively. As well-known, TiO2 exhibited low photocatalytic activity in aqueous solution under visible light due to low visible light absorption ability. However, here, the TiO2 showed ultra-effective photocatalytic activity for degradation dye via an organic molecular co-catalyst under visible light irradiation. The SDR of OA@TiO2 was about 4.17 mg/g·min, which was larger than that of OA@g-C3N4. This result thus indicated that the enhanced hole transport by OA was applicable to other metal-oxide photocatalysts for improving photocatalytic activity. Furthermore, it also indicated that the photocatalytic activity could be further improved by optimizing photocatalyst for various photocatalytic applications.

As above results and discussion, the photocatalytic mechanism of the pure g-C3N4 and OA@g-C3N4 photocatalysts was compared and shown in following. Firstly, the OA could accelerate hole transfer of photocatalyst, suppressing recombination of photogenerated electron-hole pairs as shown in Scheme 1 . It could effectively improve separation efficiency of photogenerated electron-hole pairs. Secondly, the OA provided a photoredox reaction pathway for degradation of organic dyes. The OA adsorbed on surface of photocatalyst easily and quickly formed reversible OA−/
OA2− radicals under visible light irradiation [49], accelerating photoredox reaction as shown in Scheme 1. The new photoredox reaction may be also responsible for the ultra-fast photocatalytic degradation rate and short photocatalytic time of photocatalyst with OA co-catalyst. According to above discussion, it demonstrated that it did not only consider usage efficiency of visible light and separation efficiency of photogenerated electron-hole pairs, but also consider content of reaction radicals and rate of photoredox reaction when designing co-catalysts.

Overall, here, an efficient organic co-catalyst OA was proposed for significantly improving photocatalytic activity of traditional photocatalyst towards degradation of organic dyes by accelerating hole transfer and photoredox reaction. Impressively, the photocatalytic system exhibited the highest photocatalytic activity comparing to other photocatalysts, including the catalysts based on noble-metals. Altogether, the organic co-catalytic system developed in this work provides a new strategy for designing highly efficient photocatalyst for degradation of dye pollutants, and hence opens a rational and cost-effective avenue for tuning photocatalytic activity.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are grateful for the support of the National Natural Science Foundation of China under grants (51773184), and the Shanxi Provincial Natural Science Foundation of China (201701D121046).
